items.q: When does an electron behave as a wave?
items.correct: In experiments that probe wave properties (e.g. diffraction through a crystal); in particle-like experiments it behaves as a particle.
items.misconception: Electrons are always waves and only look like particles at low energy.
items.random_wrong: Electrons are sometimes waves and sometimes particles depending on the temperature of the apparatus.
items.why: Students seek a single 'true' description; the modern view is that the experimental context selects which behaviour is observed.

items.q: What determines the de Broglie wavelength of a particle?
items.correct: Its momentum (lambda = h/p) — heavier or faster particles have shorter de Broglie wavelengths.
items.misconception: Its kinetic energy alone, with no dependence on mass.
items.why: Students think the wavelength is a fixed property of the particle type, missing that it depends on momentum and so on speed and mass together.

items.q: What determines the energy of a single photon?
items.correct: Its frequency (E = hf) — only frequency, not intensity.
items.misconception: A brighter light has higher-energy photons because intensity makes each photon more energetic.
items.random_wrong: Photon energy depends on how fast the photon is moving relative to the source.
items.why: Students conflate intensity (number of photons) with photon energy (frequency-dependent); this is why dim UV is more dangerous than bright red.

items.q: Electron diffraction shows electrons behave as:
items.why: It's Both waves and particles. “Particles only” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The real figure is 0.0001. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
